failure_surface: All test cases failed (test_case_results: [0]), indicating the formulas written to cells F2:F5 did not meet evaluation criteria despite claiming successful execution.
root_cause: Agent generated formulas that checked for 'Pass' in column C but may have referenced wrong source data or column alignment. Without first reading/understanding the actual worksheet structure, the hardcoded assumption about which columns contain 'Name', 'Result', and 'Session ID' led to logically incorrect formulas being inserted.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before writing formulas to target cells, the agent should inspect the relevant worksheet's actual column headers and data layout to verify which columns contain the referenced fields (e.g., Name column, Result/pass column, Session ID column).
evidence: Test case results show all 0s, indicating complete failure to produce expected output
Agent claimed '[Code executed successfully]' while evaluations returned 0
Formula uses '=IF(C{}="Pass",A{},"")' with hardcoded column references without verifying column positions
No workbook inspection occurred before generating formulas targeting F2:F5

memory_items.title: Verify Worksheet Structure Before Formula Generation
memory_items.lesson: When implementing formulas that reference specific columns, always read or inspect the worksheet to confirm actual column positions and header names instead of assuming layout from instructions alone.
memory_items.generalization: Column indices and header mappings vary across spreadsheets; verify them programmatically before hardcoding cell references.

memory_items.title: Cross-Check Execution Success Against Test Results
memory_items.lesson: Do not assume a task succeeded based solely on code execution messages; validate outcomes against explicit test/evaluation criteria before confirming completion.
memory_items.generalization: Execution messages may report success while functional tests fail; treat them as separate signals requiring reconciliation.

memory_items.title: Understand User's Existing Formula Intent Before Replacement
memory_items.lesson: When a user reports broken formulas, analyze why their current approach fails (e.g., VLOOKUP looking up self-reference) before proposing an alternative solution.
memory_items.generalization: Diagnosing the original failure mode ensures the replacement formula addresses the actual problem rather than creating new mismatches.

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Verify Worksheet Structure Before Formula Generation
When implementing formulas that reference specific columns, always read or inspect the worksheet to confirm actual column positions and header names instead of assuming layout from instructions alone. Column indices and header mappings vary across spreadsheets; verify them programmatically before hardcoding cell references.

### Cross-Check Execution Success Against Test Results
Do not assume a task succeeded based solely on code execution messages; validate outcomes against explicit test/evaluation criteria before confirming completion. Execution messages may report success while functional tests fail; treat them as separate signals requiring reconciliation.

### Understand User's Existing Formula Intent Before Replacement
When a user reports broken formulas, analyze why their current approach fails (e.g., VLOOKUP looking up self-reference) before proposing an alternative solution. Diagnosing the original failure mode ensures the replacement formula addresses the actual problem rather than creating new mismatches.
